danger_signals.visual_form: game pieces accumulating toward top of screen, eventually trapping Toad
danger_signals.meaning: imminent failure/game over state — player has insufficient time to clear field

danger_signals.visual_form: ceiling-disguised Thwomp descending rows, compressing play space
danger_signals.meaning: increasing difficulty and spatial pressure — play field becoming more constrained

danger_signals.visual_form: increasing number and density of colored monsters in play field across rounds
danger_signals.meaning: escalating challenge intensity — progression to harder levels

danger_signals.visual_form: colored monster sprites scattered throughout play field
danger_signals.meaning: objects requiring destruction; obstacles to clearing the level

safety_signals.visual_form: colored bombs falling from top of screen, sprite dropping them into field
safety_signals.meaning: player has tools to make matches and destroy monsters — agency and progress

safety_signals.visual_form: empty or nearly empty game grid
safety_signals.meaning: level completion; advancement to next round with increased challenge

safety_signals.visual_form: player character Toad able to move freely within field, pick up and position objects
safety_signals.meaning: player control and manipulation power — ability to rearrange and strategize

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Toad (player character, representative of peaceful faction)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: The 'Peaceful Woods' setting — once a peaceful area for sprites and gentle creatures, now invaded and 'turned upside-down' by Wario's occupation

faction_indicators.color_identity: color-coded monsters (red, blue, yellow, green variants implied by matching mechanic)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Wario as antagonist/faction leader; diverse monster types (unnamed variants requiring different destruction methods)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: different monster types with distinct destruction requirements (e.g., some requiring two matches in succession, others diagonal matches)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: monsters invading and dominating the Peaceful Woods; Wario has taken control of the forest

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Peaceful Woods occupied by Wario and monsters
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: play field filled with colored monsters representing Wario's invading force
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Toad must clear monsters by matching them with bombs to restore peace and return forest to sprites

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: initial monster count increases with each round; Thwomp may descend to compress play space
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player must work faster and more strategically to clear expanding monster populations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: play field filling to brim with uncleared objects, Toad becoming trapped
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: game over; level must be restarted

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: play field cleared of all monsters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: advancement to next round; restoration of small portion of forest to peaceful state

environmental_storytelling.example: The Peaceful Woods, once safe for sprites and gentle creatures, has been 'turned upside-down' by Wario's invasion — visual domination of play field by Wario's monsters communicates occupation and displacement
Colored monsters and bombs serve as visual proxies for the ideological conflict — player must match bombs with monsters to 'destroy' the invading force and restore peace
Thwomp descending and monster count increasing across rounds visually communicates the worsening situation — Wario's control tightening, stakes rising
Toad physically inside the play field (not manipulating from outside like Tetris) conveys direct, personal engagement in reclaiming the forest — Toad as active resistance to occupation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: moves within play field, picks up individual objects or stacks, places them elsewhere, kicks objects across field, climbs stacks to reach specific objects — full agency in spatial manipulation

stationary until matched with bombs; different types require specific destruction methods (some requiring rapid successive matches, others diagonal placement) — implies varied threat levels and tactical complexity

entity_behavioral_tells.state: falling from top, available for placement
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: fall into field from above via sprite; must be positioned to form matches with three or more objects (at least one bomb) in rows — represent player's tools for progress
moves downward, compressing available play space; disguises itself as ceiling to create surprise pressure — represents environmental hazard/difficulty escalation
implied presence through monster army and forest occupation; defeated through clearing all monsters in progression — represents meta-antagonist of narrative

notes: Wario's Woods uses color-matching puzzle mechanics as a visual language for conflict resolution. The faction divide (Peaceful Woods sprites vs. Wario's monster army) is communicated entirely through play field composition and object types. Danger manifests as spatial constraint (overflow) and increasing density of threats (monsters, Thwomp). Safety/progress manifests as cleared space and bomb availability. The narrative of forest occupation and liberation is thin but conveyed through setting description and objective framing — the gameplay itself *is* the story of resistance. Multiplayer mode ('VS') adds social/competitive dimension where players weaponize matches to stack enemies on opponent's field, extending conflict metaphor to player-vs-player domain.
